data.title: Beam Therapeutics: A Cautious Hold On A Base Editing Pioneer
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered Beam Therapeutics (NASDAQ:BEAM) in October 2023. My first instinct was to glance at its pipeline to see if the gene therapy developer had made any progress. Recall that the company was experiencing some delays in patient recruitment in its BEACON Phase 1/2 trial evaluating BEAM-101 in sickle cell disease, or SCD. According to the NIH site, the study is still “recruiting” patients. I have suspicions that these delays may be because there are two FDA-approved gene therapies already available for SCD, and neither product is “flying off the shelves.” So, if this is any indication of how things are going for Beam, it is not good. Its stock echoes this sentiment. It’s down almost 25% since my Sell rating versus S&P 500 gains of 37% in the same period.

In the following article, I discovered that not much has changed with Beam, warranting continued caution.

Recent Developments

The difference between Beam’s pipeline then and now is that its CAR-T investigational therapies are missing.

Beam deemphasized its CAR-T aspirations just days after my October 2023 evaluation. While this reduces optionality, it was probably necessary to curb its high cash burn. Plus, I didn’t believe Beam’s prospects in CAR-T were good, considering there are a few marketed and established CAR-T products.

BEAM-101 remains in Phase 1/2 development, and the company plans to present data during EHA in June.

Beam has a couple of therapies targeting rarer diseases than SCD, like alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD) and glycogen storage disease type 1a (GSD1A). In my view, these markets have lower barriers to entry than SCD.

For AATD, it may be challenging to get patients on augmentation therapy to roll the dice with a one-and-done gene therapy. I imagine similar market dynamics to SCD. Moreover, there are several potentially disease-modifying drugs in development for AATD, including other gene therapies. So, Beam’s therapy will likely have to be differentiated from other treatments to succeed in this market.

In March, Beam unveiled data after just 28 days in nine AATD patients. After a single infusion of BEAM-302, patients saw improvements in total and functional AAT across all three dose levels. From my porch, the data is a bit challenging to interpret, but this is not unexpected given its immaturity.

Moving on, GSD1A patients don’t have many options outside of a strict dietary treatment. The goal here is to prevent episodes of low blood sugar, which could be life-threatening if not treated promptly. So, again, this is a niche market and unlikely to be a simple launch. Beam recently dosed the first patient in a Phase ½ clinical trial that evaluates BEAM-301 in GSD1A.

Should BEAM-101 underwhelm, the company does have some optionality in sickle cell disease with BEAM-103, BEAM-104, and in vivo HSC editing. It is also targeting beta-thalassemia, but like SCD, this already has an approved gene therapy.

Collaborations with Pfizer (PFE) and Apellis (APLS) help validate its technology, but don’t offer much financial relief in the short term. These deals are inherently backloaded, although the 2022 Pfizer collaboration did involve $300 million upfront (with $1 billion in potential milestone payments).

Turning to Beam’s financials, it did a common stock offering this March, raising around $500 million. Following the raise, Beam’s cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities totaled $1.22 billion, which the company estimates is sufficient to fund operations “into 2028.”

Without significant revenue generation, the raise, at the expense of shareholder dilution, was necessary to fund Beam’s exuberant R&D expenditures. In the first quarter of 2025, R&D expenses were $98.8 million. G&A expenses were $27.9 million. Beam’s net loss in the first quarter was $109.3 million. Beam does recognize some revenue from licenses and collaboration, generating $7.47 million last quarter.

Despite the slow progress in the clinic, Beam’s future ultimately hinges on its proprietary base editing platform. The company hopes that its base editing technology “potentially enables a differentiated class of precision genetic medicines that target a single base in the genome without making a double-stranded break in the DNA."

Conclusion

To wrap up, Beam is leaner since my last look, but not all that much has changed. Its development pace remains frustratingly slow. These trials take a long time to establish safety and efficacy. When these products are approved, long-term data is necessary to convince patients to take the plunge into gene therapy. As such, regulatory approval is often the first step in a long journey for gene therapy developers.

Its decision to rid its pipeline of CAR-T is wise, but what remains are moonshots with low odds of market success. How many gene therapies do we need for SCD? Likewise, AATD has a crowded investigational pipeline. So, a bet on BEAM is a bet on its tech, and no one knows for sure if it will work. If it works, will its products sell?

As for a rating on BEAM, I remain cautious. However, I can see BEAM’s utility in a barbell portfolio for those interested in the tech and with the patience of a saint. This merits a rating upgrade to Hold, but this is more representative of a change in my investing philosophy over Beam’s own doing.

Notably, despite its stock slumping, Beam cannot be described as “cheap” with a $1.7 billion market capitalization. Yes, cash makes up a good portion of this, but it will likely be depleted pretty quickly, potentially leading to more shareholder dilution.

data.title: Carillon Reams Core Plus Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

U.S. President Donald Trump unveils heavy tariffs in his first weeks.

The current administration appears willing to follow through on its campaign promises of punitive tariffs. This involved imposing and subsequently suspending tariffs, often in rapid succession, followed by additional adjustments. The opaque rationale behind these initial actions has contributed to heightened economic uncertainty. Tariffs targeting Canada, Mexico, and China were initially presented as measures to combat narcotics trafficking, address illegal immigration, and safeguard the U.S. manufacturing sector. However, their inconsistent application suggests a strategic intent that may deviate from the stated goals. Follow-on tariffs focusing on steel, aluminum, and the automotive industry, as well as those targeting China, appear more likely to persist. The outcome of these and future tariff actions remains to be seen.

Loss of confidence due to tariff uncertainty.

Inconsistent communication, uneven tariff implementation, and concern over long-term implications have generated substantial uncertainty for the business community, which can be seen in declining University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers data. In the short term, global markets have experienced significant disruptions, leading to the unwinding of the “Trump trade.” This phenomenon drove an equity rally, elevated interest rates, and strengthened the U.S. dollar following the election and through the early weeks of the administration. However, investor confidence began eroding by February, prompting a reversal of these trends. This shorter-term uncertainty may soon have adverse effects on long-term investment decisions.

Rapidly shifting foreign policy.

International policy decisions, particularly efforts to distance the United States from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), have been significant for the war in Ukraine and Europe’s broader security landscape. President Trump’s advocacy for an end to the conflict in Ukraine has inadvertently weakened Russia’s international isolation. The shift has had profound effects on global commodity markets, affecting energy prices most acutely. These developments prompted a swift response from Germany, which increased its infrastructure and defense spending beyond the limits established by E.U. regulations. Consequently, German interest rates rose sharply, which may elevate the global floor for real interest rates. The policy shift also strengthened the euro; Germany’s actions may offer a pathway to economic growth after two years of contraction.

Fed resurrects “transitory” description.

The decline in inflation has stalled, and the resurgence raises concerns about the effectiveness of efforts to bring it back to the Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) 2% target. Rate cuts implemented in the final months of last year began to feel somewhat premature; inflation has become persistently elevated at a level that exceeds comfort. The introduction of tariffs is likely to drive up prices temporarily — the Fed has described it as “transitory,” and while the central bank has faced significant criticism for using that term in the past, it may be more appropriate in this instance. The Fed has acknowledged increased uncertainty in its ability to meet employment and inflation objectives, but markets continue to price in potential rate cuts this year. Investors are looking beyond the immediate inflationary impact of tariffs and focusing on their potentially longer-term economic drag.

Spread sectors were weaker on investor uncertainty.

Sector excess returns were negative in the first quarter; high-yield (HY) credit, investment grade (IG) credit, and asset-backed securities (ABS) all underperformed significantly. Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and mortgage-backed securities (MBS) had modestly negative excess returns. Most of the yield curve moved sharply lower in a largely parallel shift. Front-end maturities, defined as 3 months and shorter, did not move much. The Fed did not adjust rates during the quarter, but the back end of the curve responded to a weaker economic growth outlook. The curve is now inverted out to the 3-year point, but it has normalized beyond that. Future Fed actions have become increasingly uncertain.

Portfolio Review

Following a sharp move upward in the fourth quarter of 2024, the yield curve saw a sharp move downward in the first quarter of 2025. The move occurred in the longer end of the curve while the front end remained anchored, resulting in a relative flattening. The Fund’s longer duration positively impacted performance, which was slightly offset by its curve positioning.

Sector allocation and security selection contributed to performance during the quarter, primarily driven by emerging market/foreign exchange sector allocation. The underweight to investment grade (IG) corporate credit, on a cheapest-to-deliver basis, was a positive contributor as spreads widened throughout the quarter. The small overweight in asset-backed securities (ABS) detracted from performance as spreads increased. The overweight to MBS detracted as mortgages underperformed due to rising interest rate volatility. High yield (HY) corporate credit detracted as risk positions sold off during the quarter.

Security selection in mortgage-backed securities made a positive contribution as high-coupon mortgages outperformed lower-coupon securities. Selection within ABS also contributed; the portfolio’s focus on securities with attractive embedded options has continued to perform well. IG corporate selection was a more limited detractor, with utilities underperforming.

The allocation to agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) decreased, and exposure to non-agency RMBS also fell by a modest amount. Mortgage sector valuations were compelling at the start of the quarter, and they continue to represent a meaningful overweight. Exposure to IG corporates increased to a modest overweight relative to the index as corporate credit spreads reversed their trend and widened during the quarter. All three sectors (utilities, financials and industrials) of IG corporate allocations were overweight relative to the index, albeit slightly. The Fund established an initial toe-hold position in HY corporate credit during the quarter as spreads began to widen. ABS exposure increased during the quarter. The weight in commercial mortgage-backed securities increased, but it continues to represent a low absolute exposure. The exposure to non-U.S. dollar bonds increased slightly, although the allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies was unchanged. The allocation to U.S. Treasurys increased, although it remains notably underweight relative to the index.

Portfolio duration fell during the quarter as the yield curve flattened and rates moved sharply lower. Duration ended the quarter slightly longer relative to the benchmark, reflecting the decline in real rates. Yield curve exposure at the end of the quarter featured overweights to the 2- to 5-year, 7- to 10-year, and 20- to 30-year duration segments. This was offset by underweights to the 5- to 7-year and 10- to 20-year duration segments.

Outlook

More tariff uncertainty.

A presidential administration’s first 100 days traditionally establishes the tone for its subsequent four years. However, the current administration has needed less than half that time. The emerging tone reflects a strategy of expansive action taken at a frenetic pace, designed to advance rapidly while keeping the opposition off balance. This administration also has a tendency to implement ideas without sufficient vetting beforehand. One notable example is in the realm of global trade, particularly tariffs. Any individual tariff may lack longevity, but the uncertainty surrounding their application is highly likely to persist. The uneven implementation of these tariffs has made their ultimate impact on global trade highly unpredictable.

DOGE initiative likely to hit resistance.

Despite considerable attention surrounding the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), meaningful cost-cutting success may prove elusive. Legal challenges are expected to intensify, which could threaten the group’s initial momentum. The concept of an efficient government is widely appealing, but the rapid elimination of jobs — and in some cases, entire government entities — is unlikely to proceed without significant opposition, because political careers have been built on the ability to deliver tangible benefits to constituents.

The Fed’s job is now more difficult.

The Fed implemented a cumulative rate cut of 100 basis points in the final four months of 2024, aiming to normalize monetary policy after high single-digit inflation (stemming from post-COVID economic excesses) had necessitated historic rate hikes. The U.S. election and consequential shifts in global trade since then have significantly complicated the central bank’s mandate. Tariffs, a favorite tool of the new administration, exert upward pressures on prices that intensify inflationary challenges and limit the Fed’s ability to justify further rate reductions. The substantial tariffs introduced thus far could slow economic growth markedly, depending on their duration and scope. This challenge heightens the Fed’s imperative to consider rate cuts if the economy decelerates excessively. The politically charged environment adds to these difficulties; the Fed may face obstacles in preserving its historic independence, further complicating the role of its chair.

Geopolitical shift could spark some growth.

The evolving geopolitical landscape could redefine U.S. relationships with traditional allies, particularly with President Trump’s emphasis on increased defense contributions from other NATO members. This stance prompted a swift response from Germany, which circumvented E.U. spending constraints to bolster investments in infrastructure and defense. The durability of this shift remains uncertain, but it carries significant implications for Germany’s efforts to emerge from its recent economic stagnation. Should the broader European Union follow Germany’s lead and assume greater responsibility for defensive efforts, debt levels could become elevated and exert a lasting influence on the region’s growth trajectory.

Definitions

Basis points (bps) are measurements used in discussions of interest rates and other percentages in finance. One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

A credit spread is the difference in yield between a U.S. Treasury bond and another debt security with the same maturity but different credit quality. Also referred to as “bond spreads” or “default spreads,” credit spreads are measured in basis points, with a 1% difference in yield equaling a spread of 100 basis points. Credit spreads reflect the debt security’s risk compared with that of Treasury bond — which is considered to be risk-free — and they are considered to be “tighter” when the spread is smaller or “looser” when the spread is larger.

Duration incorporates a bond’s yield, coupon, final maturity and call features into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Cheapest-to-deliver terms, in U.S. Treasury bond futures contracts, allow any bonds to be delivered that are within certain maturity ranges and have certain coupon rates.

Headwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that hinder the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

High-yield bonds have credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor’s or below Baa3 from Moody’s.

Investment-grade refers to fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor’s or Baa or better by Moody’s.

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) is a political and military alliance of 32 countries from Europe and North America that is organized to guarantee their security and cooperation.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Spread tightening refers to the contraction of credit spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause a decline in credit risk.

Spread sector is a term used in fixed income investing to describe nongovernmental fixed-in- come securities, such as corporate bonds or mortgage bonds, whether investment grade or high yield, that provide an additional yield, or “spread,” over the yield of a risk-free government bond.

A real interest rate is an interest rate that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation. Once adjusted, it reflects the real cost of funds to a borrower and the real yield to a lender or to an investor.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Tailwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment’s performance. The opposite of a tailwind is a headwind, which contributes to an investment’s underperformance.

The term “transitory” has been used by the U.S. Federal Reserve to describe inflation that is not expected to endure for extensive periods of time.

U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) provide protection against inflation. The principal of a TIPS instrument increases with inflation and decreases with deflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index. When a TIPS matures, investors are paid the adjusted principal or original principal, whichever is greater.

Trump trade refers to the expectation that policies and actions of the Trump administration could benefit certain areas of the capital markets.

Underweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers conduct monthly telephone surveys asking consumers in the continental United States about what they think now and what their expectations are for their personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions in the future. Their responses are used to calculate monthly measures of consumer sentiment that can be compared to a base value of 100 set in 1966.

A yield curve is a line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. Investors and market analysts watch certain yield curves for signs of inversion, when yields for longer-term debt instruments fall below yields on short-term debt with the same credit quality. Inversions are watched as potential signs of a weakening economy and in certain cases, a harbinger of recessions.

Benchmark Index

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is composed of the total U.S. investment-grade bond market. The market-weighted index includes Treasuries, agencies, CMBS, ABS, and investment-grade corporates. It is not possible to invest in an index.

data.title: Wolfspeed's Last Stand: The $600mm Lifeline That May Save The Company
data.description:
Wolfspeed (NYSE:WOLF) is finding itself in a challenging financial position and is considering Chapter 11 bankruptcy to refinance its growing debt position. As the firm faces macroeconomic headwinds as automotive OEMs pull back on investments in electric vehicles, new leadership has come in with the initiative to diversify Wolfspeed’s market opportunities in an attempt to reignite growth. Despite these efforts, they may not be enough to salvage Wolfspeed beyond raising capital through additional asset sales. Given the challenging market environment, I am reiterating my SELL rating with no price target.

You can read my previous coverage of Wolfspeed here:

Wolfspeed Capital Raise Doesn't Mean The Firm Will Return To Growth

Wolfspeed Operations

Wolfspeed has been undergoing significant financial decline as a result of increasingly challenging market conditions. One of the factors that may have led to this decline was the decision to transition from 150mm to 200mm silicon carbide wafers with the intent of servicing the growing electric vehicle market at the time. Given the commencement of Wolfspeed’s fully automated 200-millimeter facility, the organization has begun to sunset its 150-millimeter programs, which is expected to be completed by the end of CY25. Given the low utilization at its Durham 150-millimeter device foundry, Wolfspeed’s margins experienced continued compression in q3’25.

Subsequent to the close of q3’25, Wolfspeed has undergone significant leadership transition, bringing in Robert Feurle as CEO. Cengiz Balkas was promoted to Chief Business Officer to oversee operations. Angelo Cancian was also promoted to Global SVP of Sales & Marketing. Cancian’s goal was set to expand Wolfspeed’s silicon carbide offering to the AI data center, energy storage, electric vehicle, and aerospace & defense markets.

On May 22, 2025, the Board of Directors appointed Dr. David Emerson as Wolfspeed’s Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer. Emerson’s compensation package includes an annual salary of $500k with a 100% performance bonus based on achieving performance goals.

According to the press release, Wolfspeed is targeting an adjusted EBITDA breakeven point at $800mm in annual revenue. In addition to this, the organization is targeting positive unlevered operating cash flow in eFY26 of $200mm and expects to be free cash flow positive in eFY27.

Wolfspeed Financial Position

Wolfspeed’s operating performance remains strained, deepening its adjusted net loss on the back of declining revenue in q3’25. Despite the -8% top-line decline, the power products segment recovered some ground in the quarter, growing by 5% when compared to the previous year’s quarter. I suspect this may have been the result of some pull forward by customers getting ahead of tariff policy, though this factor is not confirmed.

On May 20, 2025, the Wall Street Journal reported that Wolfspeed was preparing to pursue Chapter 11 bankruptcy with plans to restructure its debt with its creditors.

As of q3’25, Wolfspeed had $6.65b in debt on the balance sheet, 46% of which are convertible notes.

In q3’25, Wolfspeed raised $200mm through an ATM stock offering. Wolfspeed also raised $250mm through the issuance of additional 2030 senior notes in q2’25 and indicated that the organization will pursue additional funding through debt refinancing or equity offerings. Essentially, Wolfspeed’s 2030 debt agreement allowed the firm to raise additional capital upwards of $500mm, acting as an expensive revolver with an effective rate of 12.4% as of q4’24.

What’s particularly interesting in management’s statement in the q3’25 10-Q suggests that Wolfspeed had adequate liquidity and capital resources to fund operations for at least the next 12 months. Accordingly, management had been working to improve its debt through restructuring, refinancing, or amending the existing debt position while seeking new financing. As part of its 2030 notes, Wolfspeed had relatively limited options in raising capital, particularly through the sale of business assets.

Nonetheless, following the sunset of Wolfspeed’s Farmers Branch facility at the end of 2024, the organization will be putting the facility up for sale to raise liquidity. The Durham facility is also planned for discontinuation by the end of CY25. In total, management is expecting to raise $150mm in the divestiture of non-core assets in 2025.

In addition to this, Wolfspeed raised $192mm from Section 48D cash tax refunds. These refunds were a part of the Advanced Manufacturing Tax Credit program established by the Inflation Reduction Act in 2022.

In FY24, Wolfspeed raised roughly $2b through an unsecured customer refundable deposit agreement [CRD Agreement], to bolster Wolfspeed's liquidity. This lending program comes at a hefty 6.3% effective rate, significantly adding to Wolfspeed’s debt burden. If Wolfspeed fails to satisfy supply targets as part of the CRD Agreement, the effective rate can increase to 10-15%, significantly adding to the organization’s debt burden. The agreement is set to mature in July 2033.

Regarding CHIPS Act funding, President Trump had previously stated in March 2025 that he wants to get rid of the legislation, favoring the tariff strategy over grants. More recently, the US Department of Commerce announced that each awardee of tech hub grants from the CHIPS Act must reapply and compete against the other hubs for funding. Though this adjustment doesn’t appear to be impacting grants allocated towards semiconductor foundries, I believe that new grant issuances may be harder to come by given the president’s sentiment towards the Act. Accordingly, Wolfspeed had previously secured $750mm in direct funding but had yet to receive the funds.

The notes in question are the $575mm in outstanding 1.75% 2026 convertible notes. The original indenture set the conversion rate at 21.1346 shares per $1,000 in principal, pricing shares at $47.32/share. Creditors have the option to convert their notes following November 3, 2025. Given the significant dispersion between the strike price and the trading levels prior to the potential bankruptcy announcement, Wolfspeed would have likely had to deliver cash upon maturity.

In an effort to resolve Wolfspeed’s financial challenges, a consortium of hedge funds proposed a $600mm refinancing effort for Wolfspeedss convertible issuance due in 2026.

In my opinion, Wolfspeed will be confronted with a few options:

  1. File chapter 11 bankruptcy, restructure its debt, and likely trade equity for debt, diluting the existing shareholder base.
  2. Refinance the $575mm convertibles with the $600mm refinancing deal which will also likely result in further equity dilution.

Given the financial risks involved, I believe either path will only elongate the inevitable financial decline of Wolfspeed.

With the electric vehicle market facing potential declines as automotive OEMs push out EV targets, I believe growth may be limited for Wolfspeed in the near-term. There are also talks of President Trump ending tax credits for EVs, potentially leading to additional headwinds in EV adoption. In addition to this, Chinese silicon carbide companies may take up market share, outpricing the market, and potentially creating a more challenging environment for Wolfspeed. This includes 200-millimeter production by Chinese firms like Sana Optoelectronics.

At the close of q3’25, Wolfspeed’s financial position remained stressed with elevated inventories and payables being significantly pulled in.

Risks Related to Wolfspeed

Bull Case

Given WOLF shares’ “meme stock” status, I suspect any good news in Wolfspeed’s restructuring will have a positive effect on the share price as illustrated in the price action on May 22, 2025.

The organization may be rescued through the $600mm in funding to cover the $575mm convertible notes, potentially providing a safety net for Wolfspeed to continue operating.

Bear Case

The silicon carbide market is becoming more crowded with Chinese producers, potentially creating a more challenging market for Wolfspeed to compete in. With electric vehicles falling out of favor, particularly as Congress targets California’s EV standard, Wolfspeed’s end-market may be in decline, potentially limiting the organization’s ability to grow revenue and hit adjusted EBITDA breakeven.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Overall, Wolfspeed may receive the appropriate support to continue as a going concern; however, given the challenging macroeconomic environment and increasingly competitive market for silicon carbide, I suspect that if Wolfspeed receives the needed financing to cover its convertible notes, it will only prolong Wolfspeed’s financial decline. Given the challenging market environment, I am reiterating my SELL rating with no price target.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2025 Lifetime Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The first quarter marked a reversal of the trends in place during 2024, with a poor showing for U.S. growth stocks but stronger returns for most other asset categories.
  • The John Hancock Multimanager 2025 Lifetime Portfolio fund (MUTF:JLEAX) produced a positive total return and outpaced the benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to results, but the relative performance of the underlying managers detracted.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs led to a wide range of returns across the world financial markets in the first quarter. U.S. equities were one of the worst performing asset categories on concerns that Washington's trade policies could fuel resurgent inflation and depress economic growth. Additionally, worries about reduced investment in AI-related infrastructure caused a sell-off in U.S. technology stocks. Although the U.S. growth style was hit hard by these developments, the value category posted a gain as investors sought opportunities in areas that had underperformed in 2024. Developed- and emerging-market stocks also recorded positive returns at a time of U.S. dollar weakness and positive sentiment on the outlook for European fiscal spending.

In the fixed-income market, higher-quality and more interest rate-sensitive assets delivered solid gains at a time of falling inflation and renewed hopes that the U.S. Federal Reserve would ease policy later in the year. The credit sectors (including investment-grade corporates, high-yield bonds, bank loans, and the emerging markets) produced positive returns but didn't keep pace with U.S. Treasuries. The underperformance for these areas, which marked another reversal from 2024, reflected an uptick in investor risk aversion.

Contributors and detractors

During the past two years, our diversified approach didn't have the anticipated benefits due to the high concentration of returns in a very narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. This trend changed in the first quarter as shifting headlines prompted a rotation toward other areas of the financial markets. We believe the fund's outperformance in this shifting environment helps illustrate the benefits of our emphasis on using a broadly diversified and long-term approach, rather than one that tries to chase short-term performance.

Portfolio changes

data.title: Danaos Corp: Another Big Bet On Maritime Transport
data.description:
Danaos Corp. (NYSE:DAC) is a ship-owning company dedicated to maritime transport. The company has a total portfolio of 74 container vessels and 10 dry bulk vessels. This makes it one of the main competitors in this market, along with Global Ship Lease (NYSE:GSL) and Costamare (NYSE:CMRE). I covered GSL in my previous report, "Global Ship Lease: Despite Protectionism On The Horizon, It's Still A Good Option," in this report. This time, I'm analyzing one of its main competitors.

Danaos is a company whose ratios alone draw attention. The company trades with a PE of 3.22x, an EV/EBITDA of 2.71x, and a more than interesting discount to Book Value, with a P/BV of 0.45x. While this latter value could be considered anecdotal, in companies where book value is primarily determined by the ships in their portfolio, something that also has a well-quantified market price, it is a good indicator, in my opinion, of the company's potential.

In this report, I intend to briefly summarize the current landscape of the shipping industry. Many of the points in this regard may sound repetitive to readers of the GSL article, given that the industry is the same. Therefore, I am interested in delving deeper into the company's current structure and its future performance.

Thesis

To begin, I would like to emphasize that Danaos maintains a well-diversified fleet of vessels, with at most 23% of the vessels belonging to a single class. As can be seen in the case of GSL, almost all of the 74 vessels owned by Danaos are vessels with a cargo capacity of 12,000 TEU or less.

This focus on the type of ships Danaos focuses on gives it an advantage over other companies that seek to concentrate on larger vessels, as I will show later. The main advantage of Danaos's ship portfolio is the low orderbook volume for vessels with carrying capacities between 0.1 and 12,000 TEU. As I mentioned in the GSL report, most of its vessel production is focused on sizes larger than 12,000 TEU, which generates a significantly lower supply than demand for smaller vessels.

One of the points that, in my view, represents one of the greatest advantages of Danaos' business structure is the wide variety of companies it serves. Among these are large international trade companies. Danaos, through medium-term contracts, charters vessels to transport the containers owned by these companies.

As you can see, Hapag-Lloyd and PIL are Danaos' main clients, but they represent only 18% of contracts in each case. This, from my perspective, speaks not only to the company's ability to forge commercial agreements with the world's leading carriers, but also to the ability to avoid relying on a single company to conduct business.

Continuing with the analysis of Danaos' business model, an interesting point can be seen: the predictability of a significant portion of future cash flow. Given the structure of the company's service, a contractual relationship is generated for the use of the vessels, with a term of between one and two years, with the possibility of extension. When analyzing the company's reports, one realizes that by 2025, 99% of the days are covered by already signed contracts, and by 2026, the number rises to 85%. Looking three years ahead, 57% of the total days are already covered by contracts. This speaks to the company's high predictability of short-term revenue, which allows for fairly reliable revenue projections. Furthermore, it speaks to management's ability to maintain vessel utilization at extraordinary levels, maximizing revenue as much as possible in the current context.

Current events in the transportation industry

I would now like to briefly explain the current conditions facing the maritime transport industry. In this regard, it is important to consider not only the latest news but also the developments of other events that have been ongoing since last year. Among the most notable events this year, we can undoubtedly mention the victory of Donald Trump and the drastic changes in international trade policy that the new administration plans to implement.

With the change of administration in the United States presidency, a series of potential changes to the tariff regime for American imports followed. While the Trump administration imposed tariff increases on almost all countries, one of the most affected was undoubtedly China.

In fact, if we analyze the traffic of imported containers from the United States by country or region of origin, we see the relative weight of China and Hong Kong. The Asian giant represents 39% of the total, which demonstrates how sharp tariff increases by the American government could affect international trade.

However, as I noted in my GSL report, trade on smaller vessels may not be as severely affected by these controls. What can be expected is that the decline in bilateral trade between the United States and China will affect large vessels. Conversely, China should replace some of that trade with goods traded in Southeast Asian countries. These goods are likely to be transported by smaller vessels, the very segment in which both GSL and Danaos participate.

On the other hand, it's interesting to note, as I did in the case of GSL, the lack of availability of ships under 12,000 TEUs. While the bulk of production is focused on larger vessels, a lack of supply can be seen in smaller vessels. This could create a potential bottleneck. Furthermore, if we look at the average age of vessels, we see that it's not exactly a young fleet worldwide.

This not only has the potential advantage that, with an increase in transport demand, ships will not be enough, leading to higher charter rates. At the same time, it keeps the price of used ships high due to the lack of replacements. This allows the company to replace its fleet with more modern vessels by selling existing ones without significantly losing value.

In this regard, one of the points I would like to highlight is the fleet expansion plan being implemented by Danaos. To this end, the company has closed a loan facility agreement for $850 million. This will allow it to carry out the acquisition of 15 new vessels, which will be delivered between 2025 and 2028. One new vessel will be delivered this year, three in 2026, and seven in 2028, which will allow it to increase its transport capacity by a total of 128,220 TEUs.

One segment that sets Danaos apart from other competitors is its foray into the dry bulk business. While GSL and Costamare focus on container ships, Danaos acquired 10 units dedicated to transporting this type of commodity. While it is true that iron ore transport has declined in recent years, it is indisputable that the growth in tons transported since 2004 has been more than considerable. The main advantage of this type of material is that its growth is closely linked to global GDP growth. The decline in iron ore transport is primarily due to a slowdown in the growth of the steel industry in China, which accounts for more than 50% of global production.

Even so, this segment is considerably smaller than the container segment, and it is still expected that the situation will normalize and that trade growth in this type of material will recover in the medium term.

1Q2025 Results

At this point, I would like to briefly summarize the company's results for the first quarter of 2025. One notable aspect was that during the first quarter of this year, vessel utilization stood at around 97.2%, with a total of 74 container vessels and 10 dry bulk vessels, compared to 68 and 7 in the first quarter of 2024. I would like to highlight the company's management's ability to place vessels under its ownership even in the face of fleet growth, increasing the company's revenue and reducing the costs of maintaining vessels idle in port.

Regarding financial figures, Adjusted Net Income for 1Q2025 was $113.4 million, equivalent to a value of $6.04 per share. Compared to 1Q2024 figures, we see a decline, with values ​​of $140 million and $7.15 per share. Within this $26.6 million reduction in Net Income, the company reports that the majority corresponds to increases in operating expenses worth $19.8 million.

Turning to Adjusted EBTIDA, we see a 3.1% YoY decrease. First quarter 2025 closed at $171.7 million. However, if we annualize these values ​​and compare them to the company's current EV, we see that the EV/EBITDA ratio remains at a very attractive 2.61x. Looking at recent years, apart from the strong revenue generated in 2022 with the return to normal international trade following the end of the pandemic, we see that Adjusted EBITDA values ​​remained virtually constant.

Another point that, in my opinion, is very noteworthy was the deleveraging the company undertook in the recent period. Similar to what was seen with GSL, Danaos embarked on a significant reduction in net debt. This was due, on the one hand, to the company's debt repayments and, on the other, to a considerable increase in its cash levels. The sharp decline and significant increase in the company's financial health can be seen below. This, on the other hand, allowed it to undertake the aforementioned fleet modernization and expansion without major problems.

Looking at the debt amortization structure, we see no major capital outlays until 2028. It's very likely that by then the company will be able to easily handle those payments, given its current capital structure and continued revenue-generating capacity.

Valuation

At this point, I developed a DCF model to get an estimate of the company's valuation and complement the investment thesis with this model. To carry out the DCF, I made a series of assumptions that, in my opinion, are quite conservative and could allow for an appropriate margin of safety in case the assumptions fail.

Among the assumptions I used to create the model, one of the points I consider most conservative is the tax rate. Danaos is located in Cyprus, which provides it with several notable tax benefits. Even so, I decided to use a tax rate of 12.5%, much higher than the average in recent years, as can be seen in the Financials segment of SeekingAlpha.

Regarding the perpetual growth rate, I decided to use 2%, and given the expected level of future cash flows and the low cost of debt, the WACC I used to discount the cash flows was 10%. Regarding revenue, I calculated an EBIT equal to that of 2024 for 2025, which seems far from what the company expected. Regarding the following years, I stipulated 3.5% growth for the second and third years and 3% for the fourth and fifth.

These estimates show that the company's upside potential is, in my view, aligned with the investment thesis I described previously. If we analyze the multiples, we also see that, even compared to the industry, they are at very low levels.

Risks

Among the risks I would like to highlight, we can mention the possibility that the conflict that could be unleashed as a result of the US tariff hike could escalate. While the Trump administration's intentions state that the increase is preemptive and that it is subject to modification through bilateral agreements, the possibility of escalation cannot be ruled out. If these measures lead other countries to shift toward protectionism not only toward the United States but also between countries, the detriment to international trade could severely impact companies like Danaos. Adding to all this is the recent intervention by federal courts in the United States, which has added volatility to the markets following Trump's measures.

An increase in protectionism by the world's major economies, on a scale far greater than the market could anticipate, would not only jeopardize economic growth but also international trade as we know it. Furthermore, the way this could affect China's economy, as the Asian country is one of the main drivers of global GDP growth, is also worth mentioning.

I believe Danaos is resilient enough to weather a global recession, but the possibility of this triggering a black swan event is something to consider.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I believe Danaos's financial position is extremely solid. The fleet expansion project could generate increased revenue in the medium term, allowing the company to exploit a catalyst for its hidden value. Furthermore, the excellent management of the company's directors gives the company a solidity that, in my opinion, is remarkable. Looking at the company's ratios, we see that its values ​​are well below market averages. In short, all of this leads me to believe that Danaos is an excellent buying opportunity.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2015 Lifetime Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The first quarter marked a reversal of the trends in place during 2024, with a poor showing for U.S. growth stocks but stronger returns for most other asset categories.
  • The John Hancock Multimanager 2015 Lifetime Portfolio (MUTF:JLBAX) produced a positive total return and outpaced the benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to results, but the relative performance of the underlying managers detracted.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs led to a wide range of returns across the world financial markets in the first quarter. U.S. equities were one of the worst performing asset categories on concerns that Washington's trade policies could fuel resurgent inflation and depress economic growth. Additionally, worries about reduced investment in AI-related infrastructure caused a sell-off in U.S. technology stocks. Although the U.S. growth style was hit hard by these developments, the value category posted a gain as investors sought opportunities in areas that had underperformed in 2024. Developed- and emerging-market stocks also recorded positive returns at a time of U.S. dollar weakness and positive sentiment on the outlook for European fiscal spending.

In the fixed-income market, higher-quality and more interest rate-sensitive assets delivered solid gains at a time of falling inflation and renewed hopes that the U.S. Federal Reserve would ease policy later in the year. The credit sectors (including investment-grade corporates, high-yield bonds, bank loans, and the emerging markets) produced positive returns but didn't keep pace with U.S. Treasuries. The underperformance for these areas, which marked another reversal from 2024, reflected an uptick in investor risk aversion.

Contributors and detractors

During the past two years, our diversified approach didn't have the anticipated benefits due to the high concentration of returns in a very narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. This trend changed in the first quarter as shifting headlines prompted a rotation toward other areas of the financial markets. data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2010 Lifetime Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The first quarter marked a reversal of the trends in place during 2024, with a poor showing for U.S. growth stocks but stronger returns for most other asset categories.
  • The John Hancock Multimanager 2010 Lifetime Portfolio (MUTF:JLAAX) produced a positive total return and outpaced the benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to results, but the relative performance of the underlying managers detracted.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs led to a wide range of returns across the world financial markets in the first quarter. U.S. equities were one of the worst performing asset categories on concerns that Washington's trade policies could fuel resurgent inflation and depress economic growth. Additionally, worries about reduced investment in AI-related infrastructure caused a sell-off in U.S. technology stocks. Although the U.S. growth style was hit hard by these developments, the value category posted a gain as investors sought opportunities in areas that had underperformed in 2024. Developed- and emerging-market stocks also recorded positive returns at a time of U.S. dollar weakness and positive sentiment on the outlook for European fiscal spending.

In the fixed-income market, higher-quality and more interest rate-sensitive assets delivered solid gains at a time of falling inflation and renewed hopes that the U.S. Federal Reserve would ease policy later in the year. The credit sectors (including investment-grade corporates, high-yield bonds, bank loans, and the emerging markets) produced positive returns but didn't keep pace with U.S. Treasuries. The underperformance for these areas, which marked another reversal from 2024, reflected an uptick in investor risk aversion.

Contributors and detractors

During the past two years, our diversified approach didn't have the anticipated benefits due to the high concentration of returns in a very narrow group of U.S. technology stocks. This trend changed in the first quarter as shifting headlines prompted a rotation toward other areas of the financial markets. We believe the fund's outperformance in this shifting environment helps illustrate the benefits of our emphasis on using a broadly diversified and long-term approach, rather than one that tries to chase short-term performance.

Asset allocation contributed to performance in the first quarter. The fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks and a corresponding overweight in defensive equities. The real assets portfolio also helped results thanks to gains for natural resource and infrastructure stocks. This segment of the fund is designed to act as an offset to concerns against rising inflation, and it proved effective in that regard over the first three months of 2025. On the other hand, an underweight in the international markets detracted given the category's meaningful outperformance. The fund also lost some relative performance from an underweight in bonds versus stocks. Underlying manager results detracted, with the weakest relative performance occurring on the fixed-income side.

Portfolio changes

At the close of the quarter, U.S. trade policy remained a significant wild card for the economy and financial markets. Our response to the uncertainty was to maintain a steady, balanced approach with small changes on the margin but no significant shifts. Instead of trying to be reactive to headlines, we strive to maintain a balanced approach. Balanced, in this case, isn't a traditional 60/40 stock/bond allocation. We instead look to keep the portfolios positioned for a broad range of outcomes for market drivers, such as inflation, growth, currency movements, and political developments. As always, we maintain a steady focus on the pillars of our investment process: valuations, fundamentals, and diversification. We believe the fund is well positioned to navigate the current environment through our flexibility to adjust the portfolio based on evolving conditions and capitalize on opportunities created by market volatility.

data.title: TCW Global Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

A rise in volatility characterized the first quarter of 2024 as the new U.S. administration made clear that there would be a sequencing to campaign promises. Short term pain – a “detox period,” as described by the Treasury Secretary – would be a necessary step towards the achievement of longer-term aspirations, and the first order of business would be tariffs. Optimism for pro-growth policies gave way to concerns about the inflationary impact of tariffs on markets where inflation was still running higher than targets, while governmental efficiency and fiscal austerity programs weighed heavily on confidence. Assessments of both present and future conditions reached the lowest levels in years, including the potential for economic slowing as estimates of first quarter GDP indicated a substantially lower run rate than what market participants had become accustomed to. With souring sentiment and a broad risk-off tone to end the quarter, U.S. Treasuries rallied, led by the 43 basis point (bps) decline in the 5-Year, and the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) opted to pause interest rate changes, advocating for a patient approach as it assesses the impact of tariffs. Amid policy uncertainty and recession concerns, the world’s largest reserve currency sank, with the DXY Index falling nearly 4.0%.

In Europe, policy divergence with the U.S. grew, with the European Central Bank (ECB) cutting rates by 25 bps in January and March, taking the policy rate to 2.5%. The quarter also witnessed a significant shift in U.S. – European Union (EU) relations, with the likelihood of economic and trade decoupling between the two regions increasing. In a historic departure from fiscal conservatism, Germany approved a €500 billion infrastructure package and eased its debt brake to facilitate increased defense expenditures. As a result of these developments, the Euro Stoxx 600 (+5.9%) ended the quarter with the largest outperformance gap versus the S&P 500 (-4.3%) in a decade, European yield curves steepened (led by a sell-off in the long end), and the Euro rallied 4.5% vs the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, the United Kingdom (UK) faced ongoing challenges as the Bank of England (BoE) halved its annual growth forecasts, citing expectations for prolonged higher interest rates, a slowing labor market, and adverse tariff impacts. The BoE cut rates by 25 bps in February, but held rates steady in March, taking a wait-and-see approach given still high inflation and global trade uncertainties. Conversely, the Bank of Japan (BoJ) implemented its third rate-hike in January but paused in March to assess the effects of higher tariffs and international challenges on its recovery.

Against this backdrop, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index gained 2.6% during the quarter, largely driven by the U.S. component as the repricing of growth expectations and attendant decline in Treasury yields saw the reemergence of fixed income as a haven asset class. However, risk premiums increased across credit sectors, resulting in relative underperformance on a duration-adjusted basis. More specifically, global corporates gained 2.8%, but widened by 9 bps, while CMBS and ABS, up 2.6% and 1.8%, widened by 7 and 14 bps, respectively. Meanwhile, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and covered bonds returned 3.1% and 4.7%, respectively, and were the only non-government fixed income spread sectors to see yield premiums decline.

Performance

The TCW Global Bond Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 3.62% in the first quarter of 2025 to finish ahead of the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index by 98 bps. Relative performance was driven by the continued duration overweight in the U.S. and emphasis on front-end maturities as rates reset lower and the curve steepened out alongside the repricing of Federal Reserve (Fed) policy. In addition, the short duration position in Japan and China further rewarded relative returns as yields rose in both countries over the quarter, and the underweight Japanese duration was reduced at more attractive valuations.

Meanwhile, long duration positions in the UK and Europe were a small drag on relative performance as intermediate and longer yields moved higher, however, the overweight to front-end UK yields contributed as these yields fell. Currency positioning was also additive to returns, with the Japanese yen (JPY) and Norwegian krone (NOK) overweights contributing as both currencies posted strong performance during the quarter. Additionally, the long Brazilian real (BRL) position contributed as the currency appreciated, and the position was closed out at attractive levels. From an allocation perspective, overweight positions across securitized products modestly weighed on relative returns as these sectors were not immune from the spread widening. However, disciplined security selection produced a sizable contribution that more than outweighed the sector drag, with the biggest contributions coming from CMBS backed by SASB deals and the allocation to non-traditional ABS like CLOs given a stream of steady income and attractive yield premiums. Meanwhile, the Fund had been positioned for a credit-led risk-off environment given a sizeable underweight to corporates, informed by the view that spreads remained too tight and provided little downside protection. This underweight insulated relative returns from the widening that occurred over the period, particularly among cyclicals and industries closely tied to global trade patterns, though issue selection among emerging market credits was a slight drag.

Outlook and Positioning

While there has been a brief reprieve (tariffs have been paused for 90 days on non-retaliating countries at the time of writing this), the erratic tariff development will likely keep volatility elevated in our view. Uncertainty remains high in terms of the global economic outlook, global capex, central bank reaction functions, potential domestic fiscal responses outside the U.S., and unintended consequences, including the potential for higher inflation in the U.S. and weaker inflation in the rest of the world (e.g., as China potentially releases their excess supply on Europe and the rest of the world). With tariff wars between the U.S. and the rest of the world a significant risk to the global economy, our positioning remains selective, and we continue to look for idiosyncratic opportunities and attractive relative value. The Fund maintains exposure across higher quality sovereign issuers with strong or improving macroeconomic outlooks that can weather volatility. Duration overweights are focused in the U.S. and the UK, as the Fed and Bank of England are both likely to have to cut rates more aggressively than what is currently priced in. China remains the largest duration underweight as the PBoC maintains easy monetary policy and better valuations can be found in other markets. Currency positioning remains underweight the U.S. dollar, with longs against the NOK, Euro, and Australian dollar. The NOK remains below fair value in our view despite a hawkish central bank, with further support from an already strong and still-improving current account surplus, while the Euro should also benefit from a growing current account surplus and Germany’s fiscal plans. Australia is expected to fare better than other countries with respect to U.S. tariffs, while an improving balance of payments, widening interest rate differentials between the U.S. and Australia, and expectations for USD weakness further support the currency.

Underscoring the discipline with which the TCW team manages portfolios and adheres to its value-oriented discipline, corporate credit remains an underweight position given spread levels that continue to trade rich relative to history. The widening that occurred among corporates late in the quarter was the first step in what is likely to be a remediation towards long-run average levels, though those moves appeared to be more of a capping of upside potential rather than a full reflection of downside risks. Until valuations more accurately reflect these risks and sector- and ratings-wide dispersion occurs, exposure remains focused on positions in high-conviction issuers and companies that will likely benefit from Germany’s fiscal expansion. We continue to actively evaluate opportunities to add risk that is more appropriately priced. Additionally, opportunities exist in below investment grade developed and emerging market credit, with allocations focused on issuers with strong or improving growth fundamentals. Securitized products present a more compelling relative value proposition given better spreads, in our view, informing a widespread overweight across sectors. Agency MBS constitutes the largest securitized position given its resilience during risk-off periods, favorable liquidity, and over 40 bps of spread premium versus investment grade credit. Though non-agency MBS doesn’t carry the government guarantee, the sector is supported by robust collateral profiles that help maximize cashflow back to bondholders, with the ongoing evolution of structures and deal types across the sector fertile ground to capture alpha potential at the issue level. Similarly, CMBS exposure is focused on SASB deals that allow for a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor, facilitating bottom-up inclusion criteria that includes higher yielding positions down the capital structure. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted allocations, with European CLOs comprising the bulk of the exposure and smaller positions in ABS backed by shipping containers, data centers, and select FFELP student loans.

Investment Risks

It is important to note that the Fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. Government. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk, the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk, and price volatility risk. Funds investing in bonds can lose their value as interest rates rise and an investor can lose principal. Mortgage-backed and other asset-backed securities often involve risks that are different from or more acute than risks associated with other types of debt instruments. MBS related to floating rate loans may exhibit greater price volatility than a fixed rate obligation of similar credit quality. With respect to non-agency MBS, there are no direct or indirect government or agency guarantees of payments in pools created by non-governmental issuers. Non-agency MBS are also not subject to the same underwriting requirements for the underlying mortgages that are applicable to those mortgage-related securities that have a government or government-sponsored entity guarantee. Fund share prices and returns will fluctuate with market conditions, currencies, and the economic and political climates where the investments are made. Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile. The Fund’s investments denominated in foreign currencies will decline in value if the foreign currency declines in value relative to the U.S. dollar. The Fund may use leverage to increase its net income, but these activities entail the risk that under certain market conditions the cost of leverage could exceed the return of the fund, reducing returns to shareholders. The use of leverage may cause a Fund to liquidate portfolio positions when it may not be advantageous to do so to satisfy its obligations or to meet segregation requirements. This may cause a Fund to be more volatile, which may increase the risk of investment loss. Shares of closed-end investment companies frequently trade at a discount to their net asset value, which may increase investors’ risk of loss. A closed-end fund is not continuously offered. After the initial public offering, shares are sold on the open market through a stock exchange. This risk may be greater for investors expecting to sell their shares in a relatively short period after completion of the public offering.

Glossary of Terms

ABS (Asset-Backed Securities)– A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Accommodative Monetary Policy– When a central bank (such as the Federal Reserve) attempts to expand the overall money supply to boost the economy when growth is slowing (as measured by GDP). Agency MBS– The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Basis Point (bps)– One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. BOJ (Bank of Japan)– Japanese central bank. Central Bank– A monopolized and often nationalized institution given privileged control over the production and distribution of money and credit. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations)– A special purpose vehicle (SPV) with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. Collateral– Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit– A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation’s debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Developed Markets– Countries that have sound, well-established economies and are therefore thought to offer safer, more stable investment opportunities than developing markets. Duration– A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. ECB (European Central Bank)– The European Central Bank (ECB) is the central bank responsible for monetary policy of the European Union (EU) member countries that have adopted the euro currency. This currency union is known as the eurozone and currently includes 19 countries. The ECB’s primary objective is price stability in the euro area. Emerging Markets Credits– Emerging markets bonds (debt). Federal Reserve (the Fed)– The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FFELP (Federal Family Education Loan Program)– Student loans made through this program are funded by private lenders and are insured by guaranty agencies and reinsured by the federal government. Fiscal Stimulus– An increase in public spending or a reduction in the level of taxation that might be performed by a government in order to encourage and support economic growth. GBP– British pound. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Hawkishness– Generally favoring relatively high interest rates in order to keep inflation in check. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Inflationary– Of, associated with, or tending to cause inflation. Investment Grade– A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody’s, Standard & Poors and Fitch. Liquidity– The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. Macroeconomic– Relating to the branch of economics concerned with large-scale or general economic factors, such as interest rates and national productivity. MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities)– A type of asset-backed security that is secured by a mortgage or collection of mortgages. These securities must also be grouped in one of the top two ratings as determined by an accredited credit rating agency, and usually pay periodic payments that are similar to coupon payments. Furthermore, the mortgage must have originated from a regulated and authorized financial institution. Monetary Policy– The actions of a central bank, currency board or other regulatory committee that determine the size and rate of growth of the money supply, which in turn affects interest rates. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. PBOC (People’s Bank of China)– China central bank. Risk-Off– When stocks are selling off and investors run for shelter in bonds or gold, the environment is said to be risk-off. Securitized– When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Single-asset, single-borrower (SASB)– SASB conduit loans consist of one, large loan for a single property that is securitized and sold on the secondary market. Sovereign– A national government within a given country. Spread– The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Stimulus– Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Tariff– A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Tightening– Short for tight monetary policy. A situation in which a central bank enacts relatively high target interest rates to lower the available of credit. Effectively “tightening” the supply of credit. Underperformance– When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. U.S. Treasury– The U.S. Treasury is the government department responsible for issuing all Treasury bonds, notes and bills. Valuations– The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility– A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield– The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment’s cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Curve– A curve on a graph in which the yield of fixed-interest securities is plotted against the length of time they have to run to maturity. Yield Premium– Additional return.

For more information about the Fund call us at 800 386 3829.

Visit our web site for a full menu of products and services at TCW.com.

The TCW Funds are distributed by TCW Funds Distributors LLC
